coredata.dc:title: One-step synthesis of nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots for paper-based electrochemiluminescence detection of Cu2+ ions
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Ruirui
Zhu, Zikang
Pan, Peng
Liu, Jun
Zhou, Baozeng
Liu, Chengxin
Yang, Zhengchun
Wang, John
Li, Xu
Yang, Xin
Chang, Jiayuan
Niu, Huimin
coredata.dc:description: A one-step hydrothermal method using glucosamine as the carbon and nitrogen source, mixed with dipotassium hydrogen phosphate (K2HPO4) in an aqueous solution, is used to prepare nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs). Compared with carbon quantum dots (CQDs) synthesized using glucose in a similar manner, N-CQDs show a much clearer green fluorescence at the long wavelength of ultraviolet light (365 nm) and a more stable and improved electrochemiluminescence (ECL) activity. A paper-based ECL sensor is developed with N-CQDs, based on the easy-scalable screen printing technology, and K2S2O8 is used as a co-reactant to detect Cu2+ ions. The ECL strength of the N-CQDs-K2S2O8 system increases with increasing Cu2+ ions concentration. The sensor shows overall excellent ECL stability, reproducibility, and selectivity, with a wide linear relationship ranging from 0.01 to 1000 μM in the detection of Cu2+ ions. This one-step synthesis of N-CQDs provides a feasible method for preparing portable paper-based ECL sensors for future applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs)
Electrochemiluminescence (ECL)
Paper-based analytical devices
Cu2+ ion detection

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fabrication of the paper-based ECL device
Effects of nitrogen atoms on ECL intensity of N-CQDs
ECL assay principle of N-CQDs on paper-based electrodes

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram of the preparation and assay procedure of the origami device.

(a) (b) TEM images, (c) (d) HRTEM images, (e) (f) particle size distribution of CQDs and N-CQDs (where (a) (c) (e): CQDs; and (b) (d) (f): N-CQDs). Inset: (a) CQDs and (b) N-CQDs green PL emission under a UV lamp (365 nm).

(a) CV curves and (b) ECL intensity of CQDs and N-CQDs. (c) Time-ECL of CQDs under 10 cycles of continuous potential scans. (d) Time-ECL of N-CQDs under 10 cycles of continuous potential scans. Conditions used for the reaction: co-reactant, 100 mM K2S2O8; supporting electrolyte, 100 mM KCl; PBS buffer (pH 7.4); scan rate, 150 mV s−1.
Schematic representation of the ECL emission mechanism of the N-CQDs-co-reactant system.

(a) Relationship between ECL intensity and Cu2+ concentration (a-f: 0.01–1000 μM). (b) Calibration curve of the ECL signals for different concentrations of Cu2+. (c) ECL selectivity towards Cu2+ detection. (d) Stability of ECL emission under 10 cycles of continuous potential scans for 10 μM Cu2+. Optimized conditions used for the reaction: co-reactant, 100 mM K2S2O8; supporting electrolyte, 100 mM KCl; PBS buffer (pH 7.4); scan rate, 150 mV s−1.
Comparison of the system described herein with reported methods used to detect Cu2+.

head.ce:title: One-step synthesis of nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots for paper-based electrochemiluminescence detection of Cu2+ ions

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Food Science & Technology, Faculty of Science, National University of Singapore, Singapore 117542, Singapore
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Food Science & Technology, Faculty of Science, National University of Singapore, Singapore 117542, Singapore

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots was synthesized by hydrothermal synthesis of glucosamine and K2HPO4 for the detection of Cu2+ ions on paper-based electrochemiluminescence sensors.

A one-step hydrothermal method using glucosamine as the carbon and nitrogen source, mixed with dipotassium hydrogen phosphate (K2HPO4) in an aqueous solution, is used to prepare nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs). Compared with carbon quantum dots (CQDs) synthesized using glucose in a similar manner, N-CQDs show a much clearer green fluorescence at the long wavelength of ultraviolet light (365 nm) and a more stable and improved electrochemiluminescence (ECL) activity. A paper-based ECL sensor is developed with N-CQDs, based on the easy-scalable screen printing technology, and K2S2O8 is used as a co-reactant to detect Cu2+ ions. The ECL strength of the N-CQDs-K2S2O8 system increases with increasing Cu2+ ions concentration. The sensor shows overall excellent ECL stability, reproducibility, and selectivity, with a wide linear relationship ranging from 0.01 to 1000 μM in the detection of Cu2+ ions. This one-step synthesis of N-CQDs provides a feasible method for preparing portable paper-based ECL sensors for future applications.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs), due to their excellent optical properties, good water solubility, low toxicity, biocompatibility, low cost and many other advantages [1–3], have demonstrated broad application prospects in the fields of medical imaging, photoluminescence probes, photocatalysis and electrochemiluminescence (ECL) detection [4,5]. In addition, due to the significant quantum confinement and edge effects of CQDs [6], heteroatom doping has been used to modify CQDs, which can effectively increases the surface traps of CQDs and produces surface passivation [7]. The successful doping of N effectively changes the electronic and optical properties of CQDs and generates more active sites [8,9], the emergence of N-CQDs also brings considerable prospects for ECL research. Therefore, simple preparation methods and cost-effective precursors still have important scientific significance for the research of N-CQDs materials.

Since Whitesides’ group published their work on paper-based microfluidic monitoring equipment [10], paper-based analytical devices (PADs) have been widely explored as portable detection platforms in several research fields, owing to their flexibility, low cost and high speed [11]. In common reports of methods for preparing PADs, the hydrophilic and hydrophobic patterns of PADs need to be further improved. The commonly employed methods in realizing the desired features include wax printing [12], photolithography [13], spraying [14], and the inkjet method [15]. However, they are somewhat unsatisfactory in terms of price and preparation methods. It is worth noting that the screen printing technology can print in large quantities, and the ink preparation is simple and easy to operate. Thus, we adopted screen printing technology to built various fluid channels by designing different hydrophilic and hydrophobic areas on the PADs to complete the functions required for the experiment.

Cu2+ ions, as a trace element necessary for life. Once it exceeds the standard, it will destroy the activity of proteins and cause tissue necrosis [16]. Unlike other pollution, heavy metal such as copper are not easily degraded and metabolized in the natural environment, and eventually reach the human body through bioaccumulation [17]. At present, many methods for detection of Cu2+ ions have been reported, such as fluorescence detection [18], electrochemical detection [19], etc, but there are still some defects in real-time, portable outdoor detection. Therefore, the design of a convenient、accurate and efficient method to detect the content of Cu2+ ions in water has important research significance for immediate and targeted feedback on environmental pollution and effective measures.

Herein, we report a facile and effective synthesis method for generating N-CQDs by a one-step hydrothermal treatment method, where glucosamine serves as both the nitrogen and carbon sources, and it is mixed with dipotassium hydrogen phosphate (K2HPO4). Using this strategy, the synthesis and surface passivation occurred simultaneously. Following the nitrogen-doping modification, the resultant N-CQDs were characterized and evaluated to determine their physical properties and ECL activity. When used together with K2S2O8 as the co-reactant, the prepared N-CQDs were employed in Cu2+ ion detection on PADs.

Glucose, glucosamine, dipotassium hydrogen phosphate (K2HPO4), potassium persulfate (K2S2O8), KCl, CuCl2, AlCl3, NaCl, FeCl3, MnCl2, ZnCl2, CoCl2, CaCl2, InCl3, MgCl2, NiCl2, ethyl cellulose, terpineol, oily pigment, cellulose paper, carbon paste and silver paste used for screen printing were all acquired from Tianjin Jiangtian Technology Co., Ltd., China. All the experimental reagents are analytical grades, with no need for further purification. Deionized water (DI) was used as the solvent to prepare phosphate-buffered saline (PBS) and dilute. The tap water sample used for real sample analysis was collected from our lab.

The photoluminescence (PL) and ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) absorption spectra were measured using a Perkin Elmer LS55 fluorescence spectrometer and METASH UV-5100B spectrophotometer, respectively. The chemical compositions and functional groups of QDs were investigated by Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) (Perkin Elmer FT-IR 2000) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) (Thermo Scientific, ESCALAB 200i-XL). The carbon structure was studied using Raman (RENISHAW), and the microscopic morphology of QDs by transmission electron microscopy (TEM; CM 300 FEG-Philips). The electrochemical performance was characterized by electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS; CHI 660E, CH Instruments, Inc. Shanghai, China) and ECL (MPI-EII, Xi'an Remax Electronic Science & Technology Co., Ltd., China).

The N-CQDs were prepared by a one-step hydrothermal process [20,21]. In this strategy, 40 mg of glucosamine and 70 mg of K2HPO4 were first dissolved in 40 mL of DI water. The solution was then transferred into a Teflon-sealed autoclave tube and heated at 200 °C for 12 h. Similarly, CQDs were prepared using glucose and K2HPO4. Upon completion of reaction, the color of the solution turned yellow; it was then stored at 4℃ for ECL detection. The prepared solution dispersion was then dialyzed by Spectra/Por dialysis membrane (MWCO: 2000) against DI water to remove the unreacted sugar and salt. Dry CQDs and N-CQDs powder were obtained by freeze drying for characterizations.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of the paper-based ECL device
ce:section.ce:para: In comparison with the previous reports on the preparation of 3D wax printing origami equipment [22], the process employed in the present work for paper-based electrode preparation only required screen printing technology. Fig. 1 shows the detailed steps for preparing the 3D paper-based device. First, screen printing technology was used to prepare the hydrophobic pattern of the microfluidic PADs. The colored part is the hydrophobic area of the filter paper and the remaining white part is the hydrophilic area of the filter paper. Next, to make the hydrophobic ink, 5% ethyl cellulose was added to terpineol, then it was stirred, and heated to completely melt. The paper was then screen printed with the hydrophobic ink in an oven at 120 °C for 30 min to form a hydrophobic and hydrophilic pattern.

The screen-printed patterns contained two circular paper work areas (circle a, diameter 7.0 mm; circle b, diameter 9.0 mm) for further screen printing of the three electrodes. Following the steps reported in previous literature [23], the working electrode (diameter: 5.0 mm) was screen printed in circle a using carbon paste; in circle b, carbon paste and silver paste were used to print semi-annular counter electrode and reference electrode (inner diameter: 6.0 mm; outer diameter: 8.0 mm), respectively. The paper was then cut into single PADs for further modification. Finally, after folding the paper device in half, the three electrodes and the hydrophilic channel form an electrochemical reaction cell with a volume of approximately 40 μL.

To evaluate the ECL detection ability of this origami device, 20 μL of N-CQDs solution was droped on the surface of the paper working electrode (PWE), dried at room temperature, then washed with DI water and dried again. Then the N-CQDs/PWE PADs was fixed and connected to the equipment for the ECL analysis. ECL measurements were performed in 10 mM of PBS (pH = 7.4), containing 100 mM KCl and 100 mM K2S2O8 as the co-reactant. The scanning potential range was −2 to −0.4 V with a scanning rate of 150 mV s−1. Next, 40 μL of different mixed concentrations of Cu2+ ion PBS solution was dropped onto the modified electrode microcell.

The general morphologies of the CQDs and N-CQDs are shown in Fig. 2 . The TEM images of the N-CQDs and CQDs revealed that their size distribution was relatively uniform in a low-contrast background (Fig. 2a, b). The inset photograph of Fig. 2a, b also show the green PL emission under a UV lamp (365 nm), and N-CQDs produced a brighter emission. The HRTEM images (Fig. 2c, d) show lattice fringes with good resolution, and a lattice spacing of 0.21 nm, corresponding to the (100) diffraction surface of graphitic carbon [24]. The measurement of particle size distribution revealed the average nanoparticle sizes; CQDs being approximately 5 nm in size, while the N-CQDs being concentrated at approximately 4 nm (Fig. 2e, f). It can be concluded that the particle size distribution of the N-CQDs was more uniform and their sizes were smaller, which was consistent with the TEM characterization results.

UV–Vis absorption and PL emission spectra were studied to further explore the optical properties of N-CQDs. The UV–Vis spectra of the aqueous solutions of CQDs and N-CQDs in Fig. S1 show the absorption bands at approximately 210 nm. CQDs and N-CQDs showed PL emission associated with excitation. Increasing the excitation wavelength from 430 nm to 470 nm (Fig. S1a, b) caused a red shift from 509 to 524 nm in the PL emission peak of the CQDs (Fig. S1a). The N-CQDs showed a maximum emission peak at 466 nm, the emission wavelength peak had no obvious red shift with increasing of excitation wavelength, which can be attributed to the higher number of oxygen-and nitrogen-containing functional groups on the surface (Fig. S1b) [25].

The graphitic structures of the CQDs and N-CQDs were confirmed by their respective Raman spectra (Fig. S2a). The broad peak observed for CQDs at 1384 cm−1 belongs to the D band being associated with disordered sp3 carbon. The peak at 1600 cm−1 closely matches the first-order G band, which is relevant to the in-plane bond stretching motion of the sp2 atom [26], indicating the presence of aromatic groups and alkenes in the CQDs. The N-CQDs exhibited blue-shifted peaks, compared with those of the CQDs, which can be attributed to the increase in bond energy due to the binding of N atom with C. The surface chemical properties of the N-CQDs were also identified by FT-IR spectroscopy. The FT-IR spectrum in the range 4000–500 cm−1 is shown in Fig. S2b. The prepared N-CQDs exhibit a broad band near 3443 cm−1, which can be attributed to the stretching vibration of OH and NH [27]. The symmetric and asymmetric stretching vibrations of the CO or CN and CC groups generate signals at 1714 cm−1 and 1632 cm−1 [6,28]. Furthermore, the weak peak at 1403 cm−1 is related to the Ph-C6H5 vibration [21], and the strong peak at 1080 cm−1 can be a result of the stretching vibration of Ph -O- Ph [29].

In Fig. S4, XPS spectra were used to prove the elemental composition of the prepared N-CQDs. The full spectrum of XPS contains N-CQDs and CQDs, wherein the N-CQDs comprise three main peak positions (Fig. S3), 284.6 eV, 399.9 eV, and 531.6 eV corresponding to C1s, N1s, and O1s, respectively. The high-resolution C1s spectrum which comprised of 284.5 eV, 285.4 eV, 286.6 eV, and 288.4 eV fitting peaks is shown in Fig. S4a. These positions correspond to CC, CN, CO, and CO bonds. The 399.9 eV peak represents the peak position of N1s (Fig. S4b). In Fig. S4c, the O1s spectrum was attributed to 531.5 eV and 532.6 eV that correspond to CO and CO bonds, respectively. These confirmed the successful doping of N into N-CQDs and were consistent with the corresponding FT-IR spectra. Hence, from the characterization data, the as-prepared N-CQDs exhibited successful doping of N elements, surface passivation and abundant hydrophilic groups.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of nitrogen atoms on ECL intensity of N-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The effect of nitrogen atoms on the ECL intensity of the N-CQDs was investigated. In the control experiment, carbohydrates were selected as the reference substances. CQDs and N-CQDs were prepared in similar manner using glucose and glucosamine, respectively. Fig. 3 depicts the CV and ECL intensity comparison curves of CQDs and N-CQDs modified PWE in K2S2O8 aqueous solution (containing 100 mM KCl). The peak potential of the CV curve (Fig. 3a) of CQDs was approximately −1.94 V and the maximum current was approximately 3.8 mA. Fig. 3b shows that the peak potential of N-CQDs was approximately −1.87 V, and the maximum current corresponding to Fig. 3a was approximately 4.3 mA, which showed that it is easier to inject electrons into N-CQDs than CQDs. Compared with CQDs, the ECL curve of N-CQDs for 10 cycles from −0.4 to −2 V showed a stable ECL curve with a relative standard deviation (RSD) of 2.5% (Fig. 3c, d). To further prove the stability of the paper-based ECL sensor modified by N-CQDs, we made an additional 20 cycles of cyclic scanning curve as shown in Fig. S5. These phenomena can be accounted by the doping of nitrogen atoms, which increases the number of surface traps on the N-CQDs and the accelerated transfer of electrons between the electrode and N-CQDs. As a result, N-CQDs produce more free radicals, N-CQDs −, so the obtained N-CQDs exhibit brighter and more stable ECL emission.

ECL emission is excited by a potential, followed by a series of electron transfers at the surface, which are characteristic of the surface energy levels. Glucosamine is formed when a hydroxyl group in glucose is replaced by an amino group. According to the results of XPS, it can be seen that the doped N element mainly exists in N-CQDs in the form of pyrrole nitrogen, making sp2 hybridized. Since N has high electronegativity and lone pair electrons, a small amount of N incorporation can also improve the electronic properties of CQDs and change its electron transfer and transport in electroluminescence. It is therefore easier to inject electrons into the QDs in the electrochemical emission process. Second, N atoms produce more surface defects on the surface of QDs, which improve the ECL activity and provide more chemical active sites.

The enhancement by N-doping to CQDs is studied by first principles simulation. The electronic behavior of both CQDs and N-CQDs is described in terms of band structure and density of states (DOS). According to the XPS spectrum, the content of N elements accounted for approximately 6.22%. We simulated the model structure of four possible locations of N elements (Table S1) and calculated their energy value when DOS was 0 eV. The energies of the four structures are approximately −1.1 eV when DOS is 0 eV. From this, the second model is selected for the analysis of N element doping. The energy band structure represents the electronic energy level of the crystal structure. According to the analysis of the energy band structure (Fig. S6a, b), a direct band gap of approximately 0.3 eV appears in N-CQDs after doping with N, and N atoms replace C atoms to form lattice defects. Meanwhile, its Fermi energy level is approximately 2.68 eV after N-doping. Compared with CQDs (approximately 1.88 eV), there is an upward shift phenomenon, and more electrons are injected into the valence band, which also affects the distribution of electronic states. The DOS of N-CQDs (Fig. S6d) is greater than that of CQDs (Fig. S6c) near the low energy range (0 eV), which makes the electron transition of N-doped CQDs easier, mainly due to the contribution of N atoms near the Fermi energy level. The above results show that N-doped N-CQDs have better electro-optic properties than CQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: ECL assay principle of N-CQDs on paper-based electrodes
ce:section.ce:para: EIS of the working electrode, bare GCE, and PWE in 5 mM K3[Fe(CN)6]/K4[Fe(CN)6] were all studied (Fig. S7). The impedance spectrum included a semicircular and a linear part. The diameter of the semicircle corresponds to the electron transfer resistance (R et) at higher frequencies. The linear part of the low-frequency region represents the diffusion process [30]. As shown in Fig. S7, the semicircle diameter of the high-frequency part of the bare PWE is slightly larger than that of the GCE. The increase in the diameter of the semicircle indicates that the paper-based electrode has a hindering effect on the conductivity, and the charge transfer rate would be reduced accordingly. Meanwhile, in order to prove the repeatability of paper-based ECL sensors, we prepared N-CQDs modified paper-based electrodes in the same environment, and compared and evaluated the experimental results of 6 groups of three rounds of continuous scanning under the same test conditions respectively (Fig. S8). The results show that the prepared paper-based ECL system has good reproducibility and stability.

The ECL mechanism of the N-CQDs is schematized in Fig. 4
[31]. Under the action of an electric field, the co-reactant K2S2O8 gains electrons to generate SO4 2− and SO4
−, and at the same time, N-CQDs are reduced to form anionic N-CQDs − radicals. Strongly oxidized SO4
− radicals react with N-CQDs − radicals through an electron conversion annihilation process to produce excited-state N-CQDs, N-CQDs*, which then emit light and return to the ground state. The CV and ECL measurements of the N-CQDs were performed using the three-electrode method. The ECL and CV responses were obtained simultaneously by adding PBS (pH = 7.4), containing 100 mM KCl and 100 mM K2S2O8, dropwise to the working electrode modified with N-CQDs.

When 100 μM CuCl2 was added to N-CQDs-K2S2O8 system, the current and ECL intensity are obviously enhanced (Fig. S9), indicating that Cu2+ has a certain promoting effect on the paper-based ECL system. The possible reasons for this phenomenon are as follows [32]: this phenomenon can well be caused by CuCl2 acting as a catalyst in the reaction which catalyzes the reaction of aromatic ring compounds in the PBS aqueous solution. Via the coordination of metal and free radicals and corresponding reagents, an electronic bridge is constructed between N-CQDs and co-reactant K2S2O8 through the metal, which accelerates and guides the transfer of electrons, realizes the directional transfer of free radicals, and makes the reduction reaction easier to proceed, leading to an increase in electrochemiluminescence signal.

Fig. 5 shows the linearity, selectivity, repeatability, and stability of the N-CQDs for the sensitive detection of Cu2+ ions. As shown in Fig. 5a, under optimum conditions, with increasing Cu2+ ions concentration within the range 0.01–1000 μM, the ECL intensity increases. A good linear relationship between the ECL intensity and the logarithmic concentration of Cu2+ (X = lg c) is observed (Fig. 5b), within the range 0.01–1000 μM with a detection limit of 0.12 μM. We have also compared the detection of Cu2+ using various ECL methods, as shown in Table 1 . These results clearly indicate a useful concentration range for detecting Cu2+ at lower concentrations. The United States Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) has set the maximum allowable level of copper in drinking water at 1.3 ppm (20 μM) [33,34]. It is gratifying that the LOD of this work is well below this standard, therefore the method adopted by ECL would be suitable for the analytical technique for detecting Cu2+ at low concentrations. In addition, under similar experimental conditions, the ECL intensity of different metal chlorides (FeCl3, MnCl2, KCl, AlCl3, NaCl, ZnCl2, CoCl2, CaCl2, InCl3, MgCl2, NiCl2) with the same concentration (100 μm) was obtained. Fig. 5c shows that the ECL selectivity remains almost unchanged by the addition of other metal ions, the interference resistance results after the addition of 11 different chlorides are satisfactory. Fig. 5d further shows the stability of ECL emission of 10 μM Cu2+ ions under 10 cycles of cyclic voltammetry. The ECL intensity of the N-CQDs/PWE paper-based sensor is quite stable as a Cu2+ ions ECL sensing, indicating its satisfactory reliability.

To further verify the reliability of this study in real samples, Cu2+ ions with different concentrations were added to tap water and compared with prepared N-CQDs modified paper-based ECL sensors and ultraviolet–visible spectrometry (UV–Vis spectrometry). From the results listed in Table S2, the recovery of all three experiments showed a good performance. The results show that the paper-based ECL sensor is useful for the detection of Cu2+ in real samples.

In conclusion, a facile method is developed to synthesize N-CQDs, which prove the successful doping of N element through a series of characterization, and show stronger ECL activity and better stability than that of the unmodified CQDs. The paste prepared by ethyl cellulose and terpineol is printed by screen printing to form channels with good hydrophobic properties, and then prepared paper-based electrodes by three electrodes printed. Therefore, we use N-CQDs as the luminescent material and K2S2O8 as the co-reactant to develop a paper-based ECL sensor on to detect Cu2+. The paper-based ECL sensor is linearly sensitive to Cu2+ ions in the concentration range 0.01–1000 μΜ, and exhibits good repeatability, stability, and selectivity. And the detection results of Cu2+ ions in tap water prove the reliability of the prepared sensor in the detection of real samples. In summary, the present study provides a feasible way for the synthesis of N-CQDs and their application in portable paper-based ECL sensor to detect Cu2+ ions.

Ruirui Li: Data curation, Writing – original draft. Zikang Zhu: Investigation, Formal analysis. Peng Pan: Conceptualization, Supervision, Methodology. Jun Liu: Data curation. Baozeng Zhou: Data curation. Chengxin Liu: Investigation, Data curation. Zhengchun Yang: Conceptualization, Supervision, Methodology, Writing – review & editing, Resources, Funding acquisition. John Wang: Supervision, Funding acquisition, Writing – review & editing. Xu Li: Supervision. Xin Yang: Investigation, Data curation. Jiayuan Chang: Investigation, Data curation. Huimin Niu: Investigation, Data curation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We acknowledge the support by Tianjin Natural Science Foundation (Grant No. 18JCZDJC99800), National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 51502203), Tianjin Distinguished Professor Foundation of Young Researcher, Tianjin Development Program for Innovation and Entrepreneurship, for research conducted at Tianjin University of Technology, and the Ministry of Education (MOE2018-T2-2-095) for research conducted at the National University of Singapore.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2021.107057.

ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Phase transfer reaction for the preparation of stable polymer-quantum dot conjugates
J. Photochem. Photobiol. A-Chem.

Chem. Soc. Rev.
